tier_1_regimens.notes: Liposomal Doxorubicin is preferred for first-line treatment of advanced Kaposi sarcoma in non-transplant patients. Due to potential risk of cardiotoxicity, perform echocardiogram prior to initial course and repeat periodically. Consider limiting lifetime dose per prescribing guidelines, but patients who need continued treatment may be safely treated beyond 1000 mg/m2 [121,122,125-129]. Corticosteroid pre-medication should generally be avoided [KS-F footnote b].

trial_data.or_other_data: ORR 56% for Paclitaxel vs 46% for Pegylated-liposomal Doxorubicin (P = .49); median PFS 17.5 vs 12.2 months (P = .66); 2-year survival 79% vs 78% (P = .75) [133]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Paclitaxel is an alternative first-line option for advanced Kaposi sarcoma in non-transplant patients. Dose schedule options include weekly, biweekly, or every 3 weeks dosing. Neutropenia is the most frequent dose-limiting toxicity [130-133].

drugs.name: Sirolimus
drugs.schedule: loading dose followed by 0.04–0.06 mg/kg/day to maintain trough blood levels of 6–10 ng/mL or 2 mg PO daily

trial_data.or_other_data: All cutaneous lesions disappeared with histologic remission at 6 months; no acute rejection or changes in kidney-graft function [21]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Sirolimus is preferred for transplant-associated Kaposi sarcoma. Switching to Sirolimus for immunosuppression may be sufficient for KS control and treatment, especially if able to halt or reduce other immunosuppressive agents. For PWH on ART, consult with an ID pharmacist prior to dosing due to drug-drug interactions [KS-F footnote e].

For transplant-associated Kaposi sarcoma, Liposomal Doxorubicin is an alternative first-line option. Systemic therapy given concurrently with Sirolimus would be appropriate in more aggressive or advanced disease. Cardiac monitoring with echocardiogram is recommended [KS-F footnote d].
Paclitaxel is an alternative first-line option for transplant-associated Kaposi sarcoma, particularly for more aggressive disease. Dose schedules are as for non-transplant KS [135,136].

trial_data.or_other_data: ORR 40% (95% CI, 16.3–64.7); median duration of response 12.5 months [143]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab is an option for relapsed or refractory Kaposi sarcoma. It should not be used in patients with MCD or KICS due to risk of flare. If history of KSHV-associated diseases, use with caution and more frequent monitoring [KS-F footnote i]. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted [KS-F footnote k].

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.schedule: every 3 weeks for up to 6 months

trial_data.or_other_data: Best overall response rate 71% (95% CI, 44–90); CR 22%, PR 59%, SD 29% [144]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is an option for relapsed or refractory Kaposi sarcoma. It should not be used in patients with MCD or KICS due to risk of flare. If history of KSHV-associated diseases, use with caution [KS-F footnote i].

trial_data.or_other_data: ORR 60% in PWH group (95% CI, 32%–84%) [72]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pomalidomide has accelerated FDA approval for HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma after ART proves insufficient and for patients without HIV. Patients of childbearing potential require effective contraception per REMS program [KS-F footnote l]. The NCCN Panel believes the 4-mg dose is sufficient [KS-F footnote m].

drugs.name: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel
drugs.schedule: on days 1, 8, and 15 of each 28-day cycle

trial_data.or_other_data: All patients had partial or complete responses [148]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel may be used if Paclitaxel intolerant. Evidence is limited to a small phase II trial and case reports [148,149].

drugs.name: Bortezomib
drugs.schedule: on days 1, 8, and 15 of each 28-day cycle

trial_data.or_other_data: PR rate 60% in evaluable patients; 83% in 1.6 mg/m2 cohort [150]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Bortezomib has activity in relapsed/refractory HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma. Maximum tolerated dose was not reached in the pilot trial [150].

drugs.name: Etoposide
drugs.schedule: for 7 days of each 14-day cycle, escalate to 100 mg/day after 2 cycles if no response and no toxicity >Grade 2, and further to 150 mg/day based on tolerance and response

trial_data.or_other_data: No difference in response at 48 months between immediate vs as-needed etoposide [69]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Oral Etoposide has activity in relapsed/refractory Kaposi sarcoma. The dose escalation schedule used in the A5264/AMC-067 trial is recommended [69,151-153].
Gemcitabine has activity in relapsed/refractory HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma [155,156].

trial_data.or_other_data: PR 33%, SD 20%; median duration of response 8 months [160]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Imatinib has activity in HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma [158-160].

trial_data.or_other_data: ORR 87%; 6-month PFS rate 76.5% [161]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ipilimumab plus Nivolumab is an option for relapsed or refractory classical Kaposi sarcoma. Should not be used in patients with MCD or KICS [KS-F footnote i].
Lenalidomide has activity in relapsed/refractory HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma. Patients of childbearing potential require effective contraception per REMS program [KS-F footnote l], [71,73].

drugs.name: Sirolimus
drugs.schedule: loading dose followed by 0.04–0.06 mg/kg/day to maintain trough blood levels of 6–10 ng/mL or 2 mg PO daily

tier_1_regimens.notes: Sirolimus may be used for relapsed or refractory transplant-associated Kaposi sarcoma. Consultation with ID pharmacist for PWH on ART is recommended [KS-F footnote e].

tier_1_regimens.site: Kaposi sarcoma with immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS)

drugs.name: Thalidomide
drugs.schedule: orally, starting dose titrated to effect and tolerability

trial_data.or_other_data: PR 47%, SD 12%; median time to progression 7.3 months [162]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Thalidomide can be considered for patients with Kaposi sarcoma and corticosteroid-refractory IRIS. It is an active agent against both KS and IRIS. Patients of childbearing potential require effective contraception per REMS program [KS-F footnote l], [70,162,163].

trial_data.or_other_data: CR 9%, PR 34%; median duration of response 6 months [164]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Vinorelbine is an effective and safe drug for AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma [164].

This is the preferred regimen for KICS. Liposomal Doxorubicin as KS-directed therapy with Rituximab has resulted in control of KICS. Rituximab and hyaluronidase human injection for subcutaneous use may be substituted after the first full IV dose [KICS-1 footnote d]. Steroids should generally be avoided [KICS-1 footnote b].

Alternative KS-directed systemic therapies (e.g., those listed for relapsed/refractory KS) plus Rituximab may be considered. In mild cases, KS-directed therapy alone may be sufficient, and Rituximab may be added later if inadequate response. Because flares of KS may occur due to prolonged Rituximab effect, KS-directed therapy should be continued for at least 3 months after cessation of Rituximab [KICS-1 footnote f].

tier_1_regimens.guideline_category: Useful in certain circumstances for refractory KICS
tier_1_regimens.notes: Tocilizumab blocks the IL-6 receptor but not the portion bound by viral IL-6. It may be added to KS-directed therapy for refractory KICS [KICS-1 footnote e].

tier_1_regimens.guideline_category: Useful in certain circumstances for refractory KICS
tier_1_regimens.notes: Siltuximab binds to human IL-6, preventing it from activating the IL-6 receptor. It may be added to KS-directed therapy for refractory KICS [KICS-1 footnote e].

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous disease

drugs.name: Alitretinoin 0.1% gel
drugs.schedule: apply 2 times daily to affected skin sites; increase to 3–4 times daily if tolerated

trial_data.or_other_data: Cutaneous tumor response rate 37% for alitretinoin vs 7% for vehicle [74]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Alitretinoin gel is a retinoid option for limited cutaneous Kaposi sarcoma. It was well tolerated with mostly mild to moderate adverse events limited to the application site [74,75].

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous disease

drugs.name: Imiquimod 5% cream
drugs.schedule: apply one sachet to up to 20 cm2 of affected skin and cover with occlusive dressing for 8 hours three times per week; titrate frequency to effect and tolerability (up to once daily)

trial_data.trial_name: Phase I to II open-label trial
trial_data.or_other_data: Response rate 47% in HIV-negative patients [88]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Imiquimod is a topical immune response modulator. Evidence is primarily from classic and transplant-associated Kaposi sarcoma; extrapolated to HIV-related KS based on expert opinion [82,88,89].

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous disease

trial_data.or_other_data: CR 74%, PR 26% of lesions; recurrence rate 26% [104]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Intralesional Vinblastine is effective for oral and cutaneous Kaposi sarcoma. Pain from injection is common and may persist for several days; NSAIDs may be useful. Use with caution on plantar and palmar surfaces [101-107].

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous disease

drugs.name: Bleomycin
drugs.schedule: 0.5 unit per 0.5 cm2 of lesion every 3 to 4 weeks until response

trial_data.or_other_data: CR 43%, PR 44% of lesions [113]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Intralesional Bleomycin has activity in cutaneous Kaposi sarcoma. Local pain may occur [111-113].

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous disease

trial_data.or_other_data: CR without recurrence in 63% of patients [120]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cryotherapy should be done by clinicians with expertise in cutaneous cancer cryotherapy. It is most effective for non-nodular, smaller lesions (<1 cm) [87,120].

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Marginal excision is an option for local control of small lesions. Wide excision is not indicated as it does not contribute to control of Kaposi sarcoma [114-118].

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Electrodesiccation and curettage is an option for local control of small, symptomatic lesions. Data are extremely limited [119].

tier_1_regimens.site: Kaposi sarcoma (limited cutaneous or advanced disease when systemic therapy not feasible)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Radiation therapy options include various fractionation schemas: 6–8 Gy in 1 fraction, 20 Gy in 5 fractions, 24 Gy in 12 fractions, 30 Gy in 10–15 fractions, 40 Gy in 20 fractions. Lower doses for smaller superficial lesions; higher doses for extensive deep lesions. Caution with pre-existing lymphedema. For advanced disease, systemic therapy is preferred over RT [KS-E references 1-5].

management_principles.overview: The management of Kaposi sarcoma (KS) is guided by principles centered on immune optimization, avoidance of additional immunosuppression, and a multimodal, patient-centered approach. Treatment goals are primarily palliative and focused on disease control, as complete remissions are rare, especially in advanced disease. Individual KS lesions are often distinct clones arising from common risk factors like immunosuppression and persistent KSHV infection, rather than metastases, so treating existing lesions may not prevent new ones [KS-B]. The approach varies by KS type (HIV-associated, classic, iatrogenic, endemic), disease extent, and patient performance status. For HIV-related KS, co-management with an HIV specialist is critical to optimize antiretroviral therapy (ART) and immune reconstitution [KS-B]. Glucocorticoids in any formulation are generally contraindicated due to association with KS progression, except in life-threatening conditions [KS-B, 1-3].

by_intent.patient_group: All patients with KS, particularly those with symptomatic or advanced disease.
by_intent.approach: For limited cutaneous disease, minimally invasive local therapies are preferred. For advanced disease, systemic therapy is used with the goal of reducing symptoms, lymphedema, or organ threat. Treatment is continued until toxicity or response plateau; maintenance beyond plateau is not recommended [KS-B].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Oncology and HIV clinicians, along with oncology and HIV pharmacists if available, should review proposed cancer therapy, supportive care medications, and ART for drug-drug interactions (DDIs) and overlapping toxicities before initiation. Co-management by an oncologist and an HIV clinician is recommended for the duration of therapy [KS-2, footnote g].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status is assessed using the Karnofsky Performance Status (KPS) scale. In the TIS staging system, S0 indicates KPS ≥70 (good risk), and S1 indicates KPS <70 (poor risk) [KS-A]. Patients with poor performance status due to KS may still benefit from systemic therapy if it can improve performance status [KS-3, footnote u].

nodes.criteria: Patients with limited cutaneous disease defined as T0 (confined to skin and/or lymph nodes and/or minimal oral disease) and asymptomatic and cosmetically acceptable to the patient. For PWH, ART should be started or continued.

options.label: Observe with ART optimization for PWH

management_pathways.title: Symptomatic or Cosmetically Bothersome Limited Cutaneous Kaposi Sarcoma

nodes.criteria: Patients with limited cutaneous disease that is symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome, including PWH and those without HIV.

options.label: Topical therapy (alitretinoin 0.1% gel or imiquimod 5% cream)
Limited systemic therapy (e.g., 3–6 cycles) for PWH initiating or re-initiating ART

management_pathways.title: Advanced Kaposi Sarcoma Eligible for Systemic Therapy

nodes.criteria: Advanced disease defined as T1 (tumor-associated edema or ulceration, extensive oral KS, gastrointestinal KS, KS in organs other than lymph nodes) or extensive T0 cutaneous disease or nodal disease. Patient is eligible for systemic therapy based on performance status and absence of reversible contraindications.

options.label: First-line systemic therapy (e.g., liposomal doxorubicin for non-transplant KS; sirolimus for transplant KS)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Continue or initiate ART for PWH concurrently with systemic therapy.

management_pathways.title: Advanced Kaposi Sarcoma Not Immediately Eligible for Systemic Therapy

nodes.criteria: Advanced disease with poor performance status or medical comorbidities that preclude upfront systemic therapy (e.g., active infection, high-level HIV viremia).

options.label: Medical optimization (address active infections, initiate/modify ART)
Consider radiation therapy for palliation or short-term management

adjuvant_logic.summary: Reassess eligibility for systemic therapy after optimization; if poor performance status is due to KS, systemic therapy may still be considered as it can improve performance status.

nodes.criteria: Relapsed or progressive disease after initial response, confirmed by biopsy to rule out mimickers.

options.label: Repeat first-line systemic therapy if previously effective and durable
Alternative first-line systemic therapy or subsequent options

adjuvant_logic.summary: Evaluate for inadequate HIV control and address possible change in ART with an HIV specialist.

nodes.criteria: Progressive disease after first-line systemic therapy.

options.label: Other options (e.g., albumin-bound paclitaxel, bortezomib, etoposide, gemcitabine, imatinib, ipilimumab + nivolumab, lenalidomide, sirolimus for transplant KS, thalidomide for IRIS, vinorelbine)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Continue ART for PWH; treatments can be repeated if tolerated and response durable (≥3 months).

nodes.criteria: Patients with clinical features of KICS (fever, fatigue, edema, cachexia, gastrointestinal or respiratory symptoms, pain, neuropathy, altered mental state) and evidence of KSHV viremia, excluding MCD by biopsy.

options.label: Other recommended: Alternative KS-directed systemic therapies + Rituximab

adjuvant_logic.summary: For transplant recipients, maximize Sirolimus and minimize other immunosuppression. If refractory, consider adding anti-IL-6-directed therapy (Tocilizumab or Siltuximab).

items.text: Review of adequate slides from paraffin block representative of the tumor by a pathologist with expertise in KS diagnosis.
Rebiopsy if initial biopsy is non-diagnostic.
Histopathology review of adequate biopsy (e.g., skin punch, incisional, excisional) with adequate immunophenotyping to establish diagnosis.
Immunohistochemistry (IHC) panel: Kaposi sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV; HHV-8) LANA-1.

pretreatment_evaluation.category: Useful in certain circumstances diagnostic procedures

items.text: IHC: CD31 and CD34 if unclear whether the tumor has a vascular origin.
Encourage additional biopsy of nodal or visceral sites if a coexisting disorder is suspected (e.g., infection, lymphoma, multicentric Castleman disease [MCD]).
Encourage cytology and flow cytometry of effusion fluid if present to evaluate for coexisting primary effusion lymphoma (PEL).
History and physical examination including history of additional immunosuppressive disease or immunomodulatory therapy (e.g., transplant; local or systemic corticosteroids).
Complete skin, oral, and lymph node examinations, and evaluation of edema.
Complete blood count (CBC), differential, and comprehensive metabolic panel.
Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) screening and/or diagnostic testing. For HIV-seropositive patients, recent T-cell subsets (CD4+ T-cell count) and HIV viral load.
Photography of oral, conjunctival, and cutaneous lesions (with reference unit of measure in the picture) for evaluation and monitoring of extent of disease.
In patients of childbearing potential if chemotherapy or radiation therapy planned: pregnancy testing.
Evaluation for suspected opportunistic infections (OIs) in advanced immunosuppression.
Stool hemoccult and/or chest x-ray as clinically indicated (e.g., for advanced disease).
If unexplained pulmonary symptoms or abnormalities on chest x-ray: chest CT with contrast and bronchoscopy.
If gastrointestinal symptoms or positive hemoccult: abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast or MRI with and without contrast and esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD)/colonoscopy.

If concerns for coexisting KSHV-associated inflammatory cytokine syndrome (KICS), MCD, or KSHV+ lymphoma, or other KSHV-associated disease: CT scan or FDG-PET/CT scan and/or lab workup (C-reactive protein, KSHV serum viral load, serum protein electrophoresis, interleukin-6, or interleukin-10).
If anthracycline planned or suspected pericardial effusion: transthoracic echocardiogram.
For PWH, begin discussions regarding possible need to modify ART due to DDIs and involve an HIV specialist in care decisions.

surgery.role: Surgical options are limited to local control of small, symptomatic lesions in patients with limited cutaneous disease that is symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome. Wide excision is not indicated as it does not contribute to control of KS [KS-D, MS-10].
principles: Marginal excision is appropriate for small lesions; wide excision with goal of negative margins is not indicated [KS-D].
Electrodesiccation and curettage can be used for local control of small symptomatic lesions [KS-D].
Intralesional chemotherapy (vinblastine or bleomycin) is an option for small lesions, but pain from injection is common [KS-D].
Cryotherapy should be performed by clinicians with expertise in cutaneous cancer cryotherapy and is most effective for non-nodular, smaller lesions (<1 cm) [KS-D].

procedures.name: Marginal excision
procedures.approach: Surgical removal of lesion with minimal margins.
procedures.indications: Small, symptomatic cutaneous lesions in limited disease.
procedures.key_complications: Limited data; potential for wound healing issues, especially with lymphedema.

procedures.name: Electrodesiccation and curettage
procedures.approach: Destruction of lesion using electrical current and scraping.
procedures.key_complications: Limited data; similar to minor surgical procedures.

procedures.name: Intralesional chemotherapy
procedures.approach: Direct injection of chemotherapy into lesions (vinblastine 0.2 mg/mL or bleomycin 1.5–3 units/mL).
procedures.indications: Small cutaneous or oral lesions; caution for plantar and palmar surfaces.
procedures.key_complications: Common: pain from injection; may persist for days. Ulceration possible.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Not applicable in traditional sense; surgery is considered for local control of small lesions when systemic therapy is not feasible or for cosmetic purposes. Wide excision is not recommended [KS-D].
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Optimize immune function and address reversible issues (e.g., control infections, optimize ART for PWH). Refer to lymphedema specialist if present [KS-B].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) is used for local control of symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome lesions in limited cutaneous disease, or as palliation for advanced disease when systemic therapy is not feasible. It is preferred over excision for larger lesions [KS-E, MS-11].
principles: For most skin lesions, electrons or superficial x-rays can be used; conformal photon therapy or IMRT may be used for deeper or larger lesions [KS-E].
Use of bolus may be necessary to achieve adequate skin dose [KS-E].
RT to plantar and palmar surfaces might be useful but should be approached with caution due to theoretical toxicity concerns [KS-E].
Risk of secondary cancer, severe or worsening lymphedema, and long-term wound healing complications may be increased after radiation, but toxicity can be mitigated with lower-dose regimens [KS-E].
Caution with RT to sites of pre-existing lymphedema; in advanced cutaneous disease, RT should be reserved for when systemic therapy is not feasible [KS-B].

dose_frameworks.name: Hypofractionated regimen 2
dose_frameworks.indication: Various lesions; equally effective as more fractionated regimens [KS-E, 1,3].

dose_frameworks.name: Standard regimen
dose_frameworks.indication: Commonly used; effective for many lesions [KS-E, 1].

dose_frameworks.name: Moderate fractionation
dose_frameworks.indication: More extensive or deeply invasive lesions [KS-E, 2,3].

dose_frameworks.name: Higher dose regimen
dose_frameworks.indication: When more durable local response is desired [KS-E, 5].

approaches.name: Electron beam or superficial x-ray therapy
approaches.dose_fractionation: Various as per dose frameworks, e.g., 24 Gy in 12 fractions.
approaches.key_trial: Not specified; based on retrospective data [KS-E].
approaches.toxicities: Site-dependent; manageable with low doses. Risk of lymphedema, wound healing complications, secondary cancer [KS-E].

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is indicated for advanced symptomatic KS, limited cutaneous disease that is symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome, and relapsed/refractory disease. First-line options include liposomal doxorubicin for non-transplant KS and sirolimus for transplant KS. Subsequent options include nivolumab, pembrolizumab, pomalidomide, and other agents. Corticosteroid pre-medication should generally be avoided [KS-F, MS-13].

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Superior response rates compared to older regimens; well-tolerated [KS-F, 121,122].
mTOR inhibitor that may allow reduction of other immunosuppressants; effective in transplant-associated KS [KS-F, 21].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: For PWH on ART, consult ID pharmacist due to DDIs [KS-F].

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Activity shown in phase II trials; nivolumab ORR 40% in HIV-related KS [KS-F, 143]; pembrolizumab ORR 71% in classic/endemic KS [KS-F, 144]; pomalidomide ORR 60% in HIV-related KS [KS-F, 72].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Useful in certain circumstances: albumin-bound paclitaxel, bortezomib, etoposide, gemcitabine, imatinib, ipilimumab + nivolumab, lenalidomide, sirolimus (for transplant KS), thalidomide (for IRIS), vinorelbine [KS-F].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: ICIs should not be used in MCD or KICS due to risk of flare [KS-F, footnote i].

key_regimens.name: Liposomal Doxorubicin
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Cardiotoxicity (perform echocardiogram prior and periodically); neutropenia, nausea [KS-F, footnote d].

key_regimens.name: Sirolimus
key_regimens.setting: First-line transplant KS; useful in certain circumstances for relapsed/refractory

drugs.name: Sirolimus
drugs.dose: Loading dose 0.15 mg/kg PO followed by 0.04–0.06 mg/kg/day to maintain trough levels 6–10 ng/mL or 2 mg PO daily

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Requires monitoring of trough levels; DDIs with ART [KS-F, footnote e].

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events; avoid in MCD/KICS [KS-F, footnote i].

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.day: Every 3 weeks for up to 6 months

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events; avoid in MCD/KICS [KS-F, footnote i].

key_regimens.name: Pomalidomide
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, infection, edema; REMS program required [KS-F, footnote l].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Response Definitions for Kaposi Sarcoma
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response should be assessed at least every 4 weeks after initiating therapy, with imaging and clinical evaluation as appropriate [KS-A].
response_logic: Complete response (CR): Absence of any detectable residual disease, including tumor-associated edema, persisting for at least 4 weeks. Patients with prior visceral disease require restaging with relevant endoscopic or radiographic procedures [KS-A].

Partial response (PR): No new mucocutaneous lesions, visceral involvement, or worsening edema/effusions; AND: (a) ≥50% decrease in number of all previous lesions lasting ≥4 weeks, OR (b) complete flattening of ≥50% of previously raised lesions, OR (c) ≥50% decrease in sum of products of largest perpendicular diameters of ≥5 measurable lesions. Note: if residual edema/effusion but otherwise meets CR criteria, classify as PR [KS-A].
Stable disease (SD): Any response that does not meet criteria for PD or PR [KS-A].

Progressive disease (PD): ≥25% increase in size of pre-existing lesions, and/or appearance of new lesions, and/or change in lesion character from macular to plaque-like/nodular of ≥25%, and/or new/increasing tumor-associated edema or effusion [KS-A].
imaging_recommendations: Photography of cutaneous, oral, conjunctival lesions for documentation and monitoring [KS-1].
If visceral involvement suspected: chest CT with contrast, abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast, FDG-PET/CT as clinically indicated [KS-1, KS-4].
For prior visceral disease: endoscopic or radiographic restaging for CR assessment [KS-A].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: If progressive, relapsed, or refractory disease, consider biopsy of lesions as they may be post-inflammatory pigment or mimickers of KS [KS-2, footnote s].
If KS confirmed, evaluate for inadequate HIV control or other immunosuppressant use, and consider change in ART with an HIV specialist [KS-2, footnote s].
If after initial response, KS relapses or progresses, repeat use of previously effective therapy may be considered, especially if response was durable [KS-2, footnote t].
